ARGUED (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript). The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANTS AIRBNB PAYMENTS, INC. and AIRBNB, INC.'S Petition To Confirm Arbitration Award.

Defendant Airbnb, Inc.'s petition to confirm arbitration award is granted. CCP 1288, not the FAA, governs the time to file a petition to confirm an arbitration award in a California state court. Fairly read, the arbitration award resolved all of Ms. McCluskey's claims filed in this action against her.

order is signed.

Off Calendar Nov-20-2020 Text Ruling

Michael Mogan (949-424-5237; mm@michaelmogan.com) of Law Office of Michael Mogan P.C. for Plaintiff.
Michele Floyd (MFloyd@srclaw.com) and Jacqueline Young (jyoung@srclaw.com) of Sacks, Ricketts & Case LLP for Defendant.

ARGUED (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript). The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Plaintiff Veronica Mccluskey'S Motion For Leave To File First Amended Complaint:

Veronica McCluskey's dispute with Airbnb Payments, Inc. appears currently to be within the American Arbitration Association's jurisdiction. The AAA has requested that the parties brief whether McCluskey may withdraw her demand for arbitration. (Young Dec. Exs. 30-31.) McCluskey's motion for leave to file a first amended complaint in this court is ordered off calendar until the AAA rules on the jurisdictional issue.

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar May-21-2018 Continued To Jul-18-18 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Arbitration
Off Calendar May-30-2018 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Atty. Michael Mogan, Tel. No. 1-949-612-1868 appearing for the Plaintiff by telephone
appearances;
Atty. Jacqueline E. Young & Atty. Michele D. Floyd, Tel. No. 1-415-504-3168 appearing
for the defendant AIRBNB.
RULING - A R G U E D; The Court adopted its tentative ruling. Defendant Airbnb, Inc.'s motion to compel arbitration is granted and this case is stayed pending the conclusion of arbitration proceedings regarding the claims alleged in the complaint filed by plaintiff Veronica McCluskey. After review of the parties' supplemental briefs, the court re-affirms the tentative ruling issued prior to the April 17, 2018 hearing with the modification that the delegation clause in both versions 6 and 7 require that an arbitrator, not this court, decide whether the parties' arbitration agreement is enforceable. Airbnb has shown that Ms. McCluskey assented to the Terms of Service, including the arbitration language therein, because the sign up screen adequately informed Ms. McCluskey that, by clicking to sign up, she was agreeing to the Terms of Service. Under both the Federal Arbitration Act and the California Arbitration Act, parties may delegate issues of arbitrability to the arbitrator, including disputes regarding the scope and the enforceability of the arbitration agreement and whether the arbitration agreement is unconscionable. (Aanderdund v, Superior Court (2017) 13 Cal. App. 5th 880, 892; Tiri v. Lucky Chances, Inc. (2014) 226 Cal.App.4th 231, 240). The arbitration language of both versions 6 and 7 of the Terms and Services is clear and unmistakable evidence of the parties' intent to delegate the issue of enforceability of the arbitration agreement to the arbitrator. Unlike Baker v. Osborne Development Corp. (2008) 159 Cal.App.4th 884, 891, there is no provision in the Terms of Service that refers to both the court and the arbitrator as having jurisdiction to rule on the enforceability of clauses of the agreement. The reference to state and federal courts in California in the section entitled "Controlling Law and Jurisdiction" pertains to the carve-out for injunctive and equitable relief and, per Aanderud, 13 Cal.App.5th at 893-894, this does create any ambiguity as to whether arbitrability has been delegated to the arbitrator. Ms. McCluskey does not appear to contend that the delegation clause is unconscionable separate and apart from the remainder of the arbitration agreement, which would be an issue to be decided by the court rather than the arbitrator. But even if she did, she does not identify anything substantively unconscionable about the delegation clause, so that clause is valid and enforceable per the unconscionability doctrine. Order signed in open court. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR #8600 =302/hk

MINI MINUTES:

RULING: The tentative ruling was contested and argued. At the conclusion of argument, the Court holds in abeyance the tentative ruling and,

DEFENDANT AIRBNB, INC.'s Motion To Compel Arbitration is continued to May 14, 2018. Each party is to file a supplemental brief no more than 10 pages long re: delegation language by 5/07/18.
